skill.name: Terraform Specialist
skill.description: Expert Terraform/OpenTofu specialist for advanced IaC automation, state management, and enterprise infrastructure patterns.
skill.summary: Advanced Infrastructure as Code specialist for Terraform and OpenTofu with expertise in multi-cloud deployments, GitOps workflows, and policy as code.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: This is a prompt-only skill containing no executable code. Static analysis scanned 0 files and found 0 security issues with a risk score of 0/100. The skill provides AI guidance for Terraform/OpenTofu infrastructure tasks without any code execution capabilities.

content.user_title: Master Terraform and OpenTofu infrastructure automation
content.value_statement: Managing complex cloud infrastructure requires deep expertise in Infrastructure as Code tools and best practices. This skill provides expert guidance on Terraform and OpenTofu for secure, scalable, and maintainable infrastructure deployments.
actual_capabilities: Design reusable Terraform and OpenTofu modules with proper abstraction and versioning
Configure secure remote state backends with encryption and locking mechanisms
Implement CI/CD pipelines for infrastructure with policy as code validation
Create multi-cloud and hybrid infrastructure architectures with provider abstraction
Perform state migrations, drift detection, and infrastructure troubleshooting
Establish enterprise governance with RBAC, compliance, and audit trails
limitations: Cannot directly execute Terraform commands or access cloud providers
Requires user to manually review and apply generated infrastructure plans
Cloud provider API changes may require manual updates to generated configurations
State file operations must be performed by the user with appropriate access

use_cases.title: Enterprise Infrastructure Team
use_cases.description: Design and implement a reusable module library for standardized cloud resources across multiple environments with proper testing and documentation.

use_cases.title: DevOps Migration Project
use_cases.description: Migrate existing manual infrastructure or legacy IaC to modern Terraform/OpenTofu with secure state management and CI/CD integration.

use_cases.title: Multi-Cloud Architecture
use_cases.description: Build cloud-agnostic infrastructure modules that deploy consistently across AWS, Azure, and GCP with proper abstraction layers.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Module Creation
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Terraform module for an S3 bucket with versioning enabled, server-side encryption, and appropriate bucket policies. Include variables for bucket name and encryption settings, plus outputs for the bucket ARN.

prompt_templates.title: Remote State Configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: Configure a secure remote state backend using S3 with DynamoDB locking. Include encryption at rest, state versioning, and separate state files for dev, staging, and production environments.

prompt_templates.title: CI/CD Pipeline Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a GitHub Actions workflow for Terraform that runs fmt, validate, and plan on pull requests, requires manual approval for apply, and integrates tfsec for security scanning.

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Cloud VPC Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a cloud-agnostic VPC module that works across AWS, Azure, and GCP using provider abstraction. Include subnet tiers, NAT gateways, and routing tables with environment-specific CIDR blocks.

output_examples.input: Request for secure S3 bucket module with versioning and encryption
output_examples.output: A complete Terraform module with main.tf defining the aws_s3_bucket resource with versioning and encryption configurations, variables.tf with customizable parameters, outputs.tf exposing the bucket ARN and domain name, and a README with usage examples.

output_examples.input: Request for state backend configuration with locking
output_examples.output: Backend configuration block using S3 for state storage with bucket name, key path, and region settings, plus DynamoDB table configuration for state locking, with encryption enabled and versioning for state file recovery.

output_examples.input: Request for policy as code validation setup
output_examples.output: OPA Rego policy files for enforcing tagging standards, restricting resource types, and validating cost controls, plus GitHub Actions workflow integration that blocks merges when policies fail.

best_practices: Always review terraform plan output carefully before applying any changes to infrastructure
Store state files in remote backends with encryption at rest and in transit enabled
Use semantic versioning for modules and pin provider versions for reproducible deployments
anti_patterns: Storing state files locally or in version control exposes sensitive infrastructure data
Hardcoding credentials or secrets in Terraform files instead of using secure secret management
Applying changes without review bypasses important validation and can cause outages

faq.question: What is the difference between Terraform and OpenTofu?
faq.answer: OpenTofu is a fork of Terraform created after licensing changes. It maintains compatibility with most Terraform configurations while offering an open-source alternative under the MPL license.

faq.question: How do I migrate existing infrastructure to Terraform management?
faq.answer: Use terraform import to bring existing resources under Terraform management. Start by creating resource definitions that match your current infrastructure, then import each resource by ID.

faq.question: What is the recommended state backend for teams?
faq.answer: Use cloud-native storage like S3, Azure Storage, or GCS with locking enabled via DynamoDB or native locking features. Enable versioning for point-in-time recovery.

faq.question: How do I handle secrets in Terraform configurations?
faq.answer: Never store secrets in code. Use environment variables, secret management services like AWS Secrets Manager or HashiCorp Vault, or CI/CD secret injection mechanisms.

faq.question: Can I use this skill with multiple cloud providers simultaneously?
faq.answer: Yes. Use provider aliases to configure multiple instances of the same provider, or configure different providers for multi-cloud architectures with proper resource dependencies.

faq.question: What should I do when terraform plan shows unexpected changes?
faq.answer: Investigate the cause before applying. Check for state drift, provider version changes, or configuration updates. Use terraform state show to inspect specific resources.
